cards.front: What nerve is most commonly injured during thyroid surgery?
cards.back: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of CN X, vagus). Injury causes hoarseness due to vocal cord paralysis.
cards.notes: Left RLN loops under aortic arch. Right RLN loops under right subclavian artery. Superior laryngeal nerve injury → loss of high-pitched voice.

cards.front: What passes through the foramen magnum?
cards.back: Spinal cord (medulla-spinal cord junction), vertebral arteries, spinal accessory nerve (CN XI ascending portion), anterior and posterior spinal arteries, meninges.
cards.notes: Tonsillar herniation through foramen magnum → brainstem compression → death.

cards.front: What are the boundaries of the femoral triangle?
cards.back: Superior: Inguinal ligament. Medial: Adductor longus. Lateral: Sartorius. Floor: Iliopsoas, pectineus, adductor longus. Roof: Fascia lata.
cards.notes: Contents (lateral to medial): NAVEL — Nerve, Artery, Vein, Empty space (femoral canal), Lymphatics.

cards.front: What muscles form the rotator cuff?
cards.back: SITS: Supraspinatus (abduction, most commonly torn), Infraspinatus (external rotation), Teres minor (external rotation), Subscapularis (internal rotation).
cards.notes: Supraspinatus passes under acromion → impingement. Empty can test for supraspinatus. Lift-off test for subscapularis.

cards.front: What is the blood supply to the heart?
cards.back: Left coronary artery → LAD (anterior interventricular, supplies anterior wall and anterior 2/3 septum) + LCx (left circumflex, supplies lateral wall). Right coronary artery → PDA in 85% (posterior descending, supplies posterior wall and posterior 1/3 septum) + SA node (60%) + AV node (90%).
cards.notes: Right dominant (85%): RCA gives PDA. Left dominant (15%): LCx gives PDA. LAD occlusion = 'widow maker.'

cards.back: C5: lateral arm (deltoid area). C6: thumb. C7: middle finger. C8: ring/little finger. T4: nipple line. T10: umbilicus. L1: inguinal region. L4: knee. L5: dorsum of foot/big toe. S1: lateral foot/little toe.
cards.notes: Remember: T4 = teat pore (nipple). T10 = belly but-TEN.

cards.front: What nerve is injured in a midshaft humerus fracture?
cards.back: Radial nerve (runs in the spiral groove of the humerus). Results in wrist drop (inability to extend wrist and fingers) and sensory loss over dorsal hand.
cards.notes: Saturday night palsy: compression of radial nerve from arm draped over chair.

cards.front: What are the branches of the external carotid artery?
cards.back: Superior thyroid, Ascending pharyngeal, Lingual, Facial, Occipital, Posterior auricular, Maxillary, Superficial temporal.
cards.notes: Mnemonic: Some Anatomists Like Freaking Out Poor Medical Students.

cards.front: What structures pass through the carpal tunnel?
cards.back: Median nerve and 9 flexor tendons: 4 FDS (flexor digitorum superficialis), 4 FDP (flexor digitorum profundus), 1 FPL (flexor pollicis longus).
cards.notes: The flexor retinaculum (transverse carpal ligament) forms the roof. Ulnar nerve and artery pass through Guyon's canal (NOT the carpal tunnel).

cards.front: What is the course of the thoracic duct?
cards.back: Begins at cisterna chyli (L1-L2), ascends through aortic hiatus (T12), travels in posterior mediastinum to the RIGHT of the aorta, crosses to LEFT at T5, empties into junction of left subclavian and left internal jugular veins.
cards.notes: Drains all lymph EXCEPT right upper body. Right lymphatic duct drains right arm, right thorax, right head/neck.

cards.front: What are the contents of the cavernous sinus?
cards.back: Lateral wall (superior to inferior): CN III, CN IV, CN V1, CN V2. Passing through the sinus: CN VI and internal carotid artery.
cards.notes: Cavernous sinus thrombosis: infection from danger triangle of face → ophthalmoplegia, proptosis, fever.

cards.front: What muscles are innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve?
cards.back: BBC: Biceps brachii, Brachialis, Coracobrachialis. All are anterior arm compartment flexors. After innervating these muscles, continues as lateral cutaneous nerve of forearm.
cards.notes: Nerve root: C5-C7 from lateral cord of brachial plexus. Injury → weak elbow flexion and forearm supination.

cards.front: What are the layers of the scalp?
cards.back: SCALP: Skin, Connective tissue (dense), Aponeurosis (galea aponeurotica), Loose areolar tissue, Periosteum (pericranium).
cards.notes: Loose areolar tissue is the danger zone — infections/blood can spread easily here. Emissary veins connect to dural sinuses.

cards.front: What is the innervation of the diaphragm?
cards.back: Motor: Phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5). Sensory: Central portion by phrenic nerve (referred pain to shoulder via C3-C5 dermatome). Peripheral portion by intercostal nerves (T5-T11).
cards.notes: Mnemonic: C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive. Phrenic nerve injury → elevated hemidiaphragm on CXR.

cards.back: 1) Esophageal (left gastric ↔ esophageal veins → varices), 2) Rectal (superior rectal ↔ middle/inferior rectal → hemorrhoids), 3) Umbilical (paraumbilical ↔ epigastric → caput medusae), 4) Retroperitoneal.
cards.notes: All become clinically significant in portal hypertension (cirrhosis). Esophageal varices are the most dangerous (life-threatening bleeding).
